title: Super giant ART pulls back on passive strategy
full_text:
The nation's second-biggest pension fund, Australian Retirement Trust, says it has been overly reliant on passive index-tracking investment strategies and is now allocating more money to active managers as a hedge against a hyper-concentrated global market too reliant on a small pool of technology firms.
Andrew Fisher, head of investment strategy at the fund, said ART had as much as two-thirds of its sprawling $207 billion listed equities portfolio tied up in passive strategies, which track the performance of overall indices rather than actively investing in specific companies they have a conviction would do better than their competitors.
Fisher even said the fund had become "overly passive" in recent years, and now had to adjust its strategy to respond to extreme levels of concentration across global financial markets.
"We have put more money into active [management] and are putting more money into active in response to that concentration," Fisher said, in a marked reversal for a fund that has for years championed passive investment.
While some critics, including AustralianSuper chairman Don Russell, have criticised the government's annual performance test as driving super funds to invest passively and therefore leaving them exposed to the broader macroeconomic risks of heightened market concentration, Fisher disagreed.
"Concentration is hard to predict; it's really hard to guess when it ends. For that reason, one of the best defences against it is to be invested in passive management as well," he said.
Fisher said the fund was not "becoming enthusiastically active", but rather was moving to a more neutral position with a 60/40 split in favour of passive investment.
Meanwhile, Rest Super chief investment officer Michael Clancy said the concentration in markets could not be put down wholly to passive investors, and said the onus was on active managers to prove they can "add value [for funds] in a way that justifies the fees paid to them".
Clancy said that his $100 billion fund was more likely to allocate more cash to strategies that tread the line between passive and active management. "Our approach is to have a very small amount of pure passive. But we have an increasing percentage of our listed equity assets invested in lower-tracking-error or systematic strategies," he said.
"These are not fully passive; they are active, but in a more modest way than traditional, fully active, fundamentally driven managers."
The S&P Dow Jones Indices Australia scorecard, which rates the performance of active managers, found three-quarters of Australian equity funds lagged the S&P/ASX 200's total returns in the 2025 calendar year.
While the average return for active funds, including dividends, was just 7.5 per cent, the market's total return was 10.3 per cent over the same time.
Cbus Super chief investment officer Leigh Gavin said the concentration of investment in artificial intelligence, which has seen trillions invested in chipmakers, data centres and large language model developers, was also starting to affect emerging markets.
"Samsung, SK Hynix and TSMC are the best part of 20 per cent of those indices, and they contribute the best part of two-thirds of the return this year," Gavin said.
"They have exceptional earnings growth, and we're seeing that AI thematic is shifting around the world - it's not just a US story any more."
The dominance of the "magnificent seven" technology stocks, including Nvidia, Meta and Alphabet, of the US equity markets has also led to concentration risks there.
While AI and other technology stocks are pushing markets higher over the short term, the reliance on such few companies does magnify risks.
A correction could drag down a broader index and lead to a rush of selling, amplifying any losses and risk spreading the downturn to other parts of the economy.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Lucas Baird
heading: Superannuation and Investments
